chunk_summary: This section outlines discharge planning and follow-up for pediatric anaphylaxis, emphasizing observation, caregiver education, and a structured discharge bundle to prevent and manage biphasic reactions and future episodes.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of risk-stratified observation and discharge planning after pediatric anaphylaxis.
Learn the five essential elements of a complete discharge bundle for anaphylaxis management.
Recognize the need for caregiver education, including emergency action plans and epinephrine administration.
Identify follow-up strategies to confirm triggers, reassess comorbidities, and ensure long-term safety.
key_facts: Biphasic reactions can occur after apparent recovery, necessitating risk-stratified observation.
Children with severe episodes (e.g., hypotension, airway involvement, or multiple epinephrine doses) require longer observation or admission.
A complete discharge bundle includes a written action plan, epinephrine access plan, caregiver teach-back, trigger documentation, and follow-up timeline.
Follow-up should include an early review within 1–2 weeks and periodic reassessment of avoidance strategies and comorbid asthma control.
Caregivers must demonstrate the emergency plan before discharge, especially for high-risk children.
reference_quote: Discharge planning is part of anaphylaxis treatment, not an optional add-on. Biphasic reactions can occur after apparent recovery, so observation and disposition should be risk stratified and documented.
document_key_topics: Biphasic reaction risk and observation guidelines
Follow-up strategies for trigger confirmation and comorbidity management
